behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module-to-client handoff (request construction + dispatch payload), not model judgment. A fake API client that captures outbound payload can reveal missing/partial taxonomy labels. Higher levels are rejected because behavior text is only about request contents, not real network/OS effects or LLM output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Wire classifier with a capturing fake API client, call classify() with a known taxonomy, and assert outbound request contains the full expected label set every time.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is response-handling across module boundaries (API response -> normalization/guard -> returned value). A stubbed API returning an out-of-taxonomy label catches real failures in wiring and guard logic. Higher levels are disqualified because correctness does not depend on real infrastructure or model decision quality.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fake API response with label "medical" while taxonomy excludes it; assert classify() returns "unknown".

behaviors.description: For identical raw API responses, the module's normalization step always produces the same final label.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets deterministic normalization logic for identical inputs. No module interaction or I/O is required to expose this failure; pure function/object tests are sufficient. Higher levels are rejected because text scopes only to normalization determinism.
behaviors.test_description: Call normalization with the same raw response fixture repeatedly and assert identical normalized label output each run.

behaviors.description: On repeated live API calls for the same document and taxonomy, the returned label remains consistent unless the provider response itself changes.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on repeated live AI calls and consistency of model/provider outputs over time. Mocked/stubbed responses would mask nondeterministic model behavior. Workflow is rejected because this is a single agentic decision repeated, not a multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run N real classification calls for the same document/taxonomy against the live provider, record returned labels and raw provider outputs, and assert label stability when raw provider response is unchanged.

behaviors.description: Request construction enforces the configured token budget before dispatch (for example truncate/reject according to policy).
behaviors.justification: Real failure is at config + budgeting + dispatch interaction (ensuring enforcement occurs before send). In-process wiring with a fake dispatcher is enough; no real network/OS is required. Agentic is rejected because behavior text is about pre-dispatch policy enforcement, not model output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Configure a small budget, submit oversized document, and assert policy outcome (truncate or reject) plus verify fake dispatcher received only compliant payload (or no dispatch on reject).

behaviors.description: Provider-reported token usage for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: This requires a real provider call and real provider token accounting; stubs can hide tokenizer/model-specific usage mismatches. Although assertion is deterministic, execution depends on real AI API behavior/metadata. Workflow is rejected because only one model call step is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Execute live classify() on representative documents, capture provider-reported token usage metadata, and assert each call is <= configured budget.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is retry orchestration (error handling, wait/backoff, second attempt) across local modules. A controllable fake API (rate-limit then success) plus fake clock can reproduce this without real network. System is rejected because behavior text does not require real HTTP/network conditions, only correct handling of the rate-limit signal.
behaviors.test_description: Fake API returns rate-limit error on first call and success on second; assert classifier delays per policy, retries, and returns successful label instead of surfacing immediate error.
